speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number five. The survivor being interviewed is Hilde Sheraga.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, so I was talking about a gradual awareness that, perhaps, my experience as a child were not as positive as I like to think. Not that I wanted to think that they were not positive, but I think life sort of took over and started to shake me up. But at that time, as my children were growing up, I was still-- in my mind, I had some difficult times. Because I-- didn't have any experience of growing up with a mother and a father, at least after the age of eight. I still was not, in my mind, a survivor. And my mother was always the focus of our attention-- of my attention, certainly. And my mother talked incessantly about her experiences. Everything she did, somehow she managed to relate to her concentration camp experiences, which was OK. I mean, this-- it was her way of coping with it. And she, she certainly had a very difficult time. She had some terrible experiences. But it's not her life, so I won't go into that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In 1991, a conference was held in New York, the first ever-- the first meeting ever that I became aware of. And this was the-- children-- a conference of-- an international conference of children hidden during the Second World War. This conference was initially organized by a very, very small group of people who-- some were second generation, some with first generation-- who thought it would be worth trying to gather children who were hidden during the Second World War and see if they, perhaps, could be induced to tell their stories, to testify, to perhaps form support groups that they needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- I remember talking to the organizers a couple of times. And I was curious. I always said, how many people are you expecting? And even the day we arrived, it was a Memorial Day weekend. And my husband and I went early-- for us, it wasn't very far, it was in New York-- and a friend of mine from Toronto, who had come in to attend the conference. So there were the three of us who went to New York. This friend was also a hidden child. And I remember, then, saying, how many people are you expecting? And they still didn't know. They said, we hope at least 500.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, 1,600 showed up. And there was panic. Because the conference was supposed to be catered by kosher caterers. And being Memorial Day weekend, they didn't know how they'd find enough caterers to cater three meals. Because all the meals were catered-- three meals a day to 1,600 people. But they pulled it off. I don't know how. They worked, apparently, night and day, getting the thing off the ground. And it was, for all of us, an incredible experience. It was like, like a coming home.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember, before going to the conference, saying to my friend-- and I was very nervous. I said, do you feel any tension? Are you tense about attending this conference? She said, yeah. And we said, why are you tense? Why are you nervous? And we didn't know. I guess it was, perhaps, the fear of opening up this whole, you know, year in our lives, and bringing it out, and being afraid that-- I didn't know what would happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- I said it was like a coming home experience. Because for the first time, I felt that I met my relatives. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] These people, many had horrible stories, far worse than mine. I, I felt a real affinity with them [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and a real closeness that I had rarely experienced in my life. And I think everyone felt that way. It was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a feeling that, I think, one can't replicate. I, I have attended two other international conferences organized by the same group, the-- one in Israel, and one in Belgium. And it-- you can't replicate that initial meeting that we had. And I think everyone agreed that the first one was a real high. I think we were drunk on just being together, being able to talk, being able to, you know, air our feelings and somehow try and come to grips with our lives, even at this late rather than early stage in our lives, since most of us were already grandmothers by then-- grandparents, grandmothers, grandfathers. Certainly, there were as many men as women. And as I say, even though I considered my story on a scale of one to 10, a 10, nine and 1/2 certainly, in terms of enduring, I mean, I was very lucky. I think my experiences were far better than most children who were hidden. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I then became involved with organizing a group in Connecticut of children hidden during the Second World War. We call ourselves Holocaust Child Survivors. We do not discriminate against children who were, perhaps, not in hiding, but children who might have survived the camps, or who might even have come over here at a very young age, but suffered a traumatic loss of relatives, and who have the need to associate with other children, other individuals, who, as children, were involved in the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we call ourselves Holocaust Child Survivors. We have monthly meetings. And we have yearly conferences. We do speaking. We speak to groups about our experiences. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And some of us have become very close, closer than our friends that we thought we were very close to. It's a very strange, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it's a very strange phenomenon, I think, since it's not easy, in later years, to make friends. I mean, it's much easier to make friends when you're younger and you have children. And you have school that you talk about. And you have, you know, all kinds of things in common. I mean, we-- our commonality is our Holocaust experience, and also the feeling that we are outsiders and that no one but other survivors can understand us. I just read an article recently in magazine about-- somebody called Weiss-- I don't remember his first name-- who talks about the Jews being outsiders. And I was thinking, if Jews are outsiders, I am outside the outsiders. I am more outside than, than, than American-born Jews are, if they consider themselves outsiders. And I, I do not see Jews in this country, necessarily, in that light. But I do see myself definitely as an outsider. And I often think of people complaining that-- oh, I don't know, that, you know, things go wrong in the house. And I think, what a trivial thing to worry about. The chair is uncomfortable, or the table is too high. Or, you know, I think everything is so trivial.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these are the things that so many people place importance on. You know, the soup isn't hot enough. And I always think, well, you have food. Why do you worry about whether it's hot or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is a typical, I think, survivor's reaction. So that, you know, this certainly is not in any way unusual. But it is unusual in a society which is basically composed of non-survivors, fortunately.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my, my-- you know, I felt very good about bringing my story out, talking about it. I feel much more comfortable with myself in general because I have joined these kinds of groups, and because I've had the opportunity to talk to other groups about my experiences, both Jewish and non-Jewish groups. And I've always had very-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] audiences-- if you call it that.

speaker: interviewee
text: The groups that I've spoken to have always been very interested in my story. And I always feel that it's important in talking to, especially young people, about the Holocaus, that we do not leave them in a-- with a negative mood, or in a negative, in a negative feeling about suffering which is real. I feel that it's important that we give the younger generation some hope, that we ourselves have some hope, which we do. Otherwise, I don't think any of us would have chosen to live. I always talk about not only the rescuers, but I talk about caring about each other. You know, help your neighbor. Don't worry about the color of his skin, or the shape of his eyes, or what kind of a home he comes from. Just touch him. Talk to him or her. Do something that is positive. You know, if, if, if prejudice is to be eradicated, we have to begin in a small way. We can't all of us save Bosnia. I think it's beyond our power at this point. But we can do small things. And I think that's why I became interested, when I came to Stanford, in working with refugees, and working with them 24 hours a day. I think that's why I was nominated community leader of the year by the which is our local newspaper. It's, it's, it's the-- not a Jewish paper, it's the regular, daily-- the Stanford daily is called the And every year, they choose somebody to be nominated as the community leader of the year. And it was a tremendous honor for me to be nominated and have my picture splashed all over the front page of the supplement. I, I wasn't aware, at the time, that-- again, this, this was before, before 1991. And I wasn't aware of why I was doing it. And it only occurred to me lately that I threw myself in that work like a lunatic, literally giving up everything else, simply because I think I was rescuing myself or those who, who, you know, who were not rescued and who should have been rescued. I don't know. It's just an analysis. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But we have-- we don't learn from the Holocaust, definitely not. But we do learn from prejudice. And I think it behooves us to, to spread that message. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I certainly always do. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Perhaps anything else you would like to know? Well, I do know that you have been interviewed before, and that you have spoken a great deal, and that your thoughts have grown and changed through the years. And just on the basis of that, is there anything that you want to add in the telling of your story, in the, in the recalling of all these painful memories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, perhaps I'd just like to allude to the fact that I'm still angry at how little the free world did for us, how the myth went around for many, many years that nobody knew what was going on, which is not true. It's finally-- you know, the newspapers have finally been posted. You know, Holocaust Memorial Museum, and everybody knows that the story was very well-known in this country. Certainly, the Jewish community knew about it, and the non-Jewish community, and the government. People risked their lives to come here and talk to the powers that be, including Roosevelt. And nothing was done. I, I understand it. And I don't understand it. Perhaps I, I can't understand it. I can't understand how people can let their brothers and sisters die, be burned and gassed, and not intervene. I believe it was in late '41 that the story of the concentration camps was very well-known in this country, yet nothing was Done. When, in 1944, the Hungarian Jews who had not yet been arrested, were arrested and sent to the camps, someone came over here. Rudolf Vrba, V-R-B-A, came over here to warn, to ask our government to do something like drop leaflets. Tell the Hungarian Jews what will happen to them if they allow themselves to be arrested. And the United States wouldn't even do that much. And that would have been very easy for them to do. I don't think anyone was asking them, at that time, to bomb the rail. Yeah, at that time, they were. But in any case, that's another whole story that bothers me a lot. It bothers me that-- I'm very much bothered by the potential of man's inhumanity to man. I think about that a lot, by the evil that takes place in the world, that man is capable of so, so much evil, and that most people ignore it, and even condone it. Excuse it. I don't, I don't have the answers. I only have the questions. But I know that we have to go on fighting for a better world, even though it may not be possible to achieve it. I, you know, I'm not very optimistic.

speaker: interviewer
text: These are just some of the thoughts that I-- you know, I wish I was a little more positive. I am, otherwise I wouldn't be around. I think that's, that's the other side of the coin. Life has so many beautiful possibilities. There's so many brilliant people around who have so much potential. Why isn't all that potential put to good use, rather than to evil use?

speaker: interviewee
text: It just, you know, boggles my mind and will always do so. I will never understand how the Holocaust could have taken place over so many years. That was such a long time. And it was known that it would happen. And how come that men were able to take part in it, and other men were able to stand by and allow it to happen, I will never understand that, with or without a God, which doesn't really come into it, as far as I'm concerned. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think maybe I've said enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of my parents when they were engaged, My father on the left, Adolf Strauss, my mother on the right, Bella, maiden name Lehmann, Strauss. It was taken in 1926. They were married in June of '26. And-- This is a photograph of my father with myself on the left and my brother on the right. It was taken in 1938, shortly before we left. I believe that that's the dress I wore when I left Germany. And as you can see, I look like a fairly happy child, and my brother, a very handsome young man. He was 10 there. I was eight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Martin, M-A-R-T-I-N. This is a photograph of the orphanage in Brussels, where I spent eight years of my life. It appears-- and it was, what I believe to be, a very beautiful-looking institution. The facilities were comfortable. The, the place was clean and neat. As you can see, we had lawns. And we had trees. And they were very well taken care of. There were gardeners there. The lawns were always manicured. And the place always looked very spotless. And I have very fond memories. I've gone back several times. Unfortunately, the place is now very rundown. They say you can't go home again. Indeed, that is the case. The lawns are no longer taken-- being taken care of. The-- no one has painted the facility. And it's very run down. It still is being used as a facility for children who need special daycare attention. This is not daycare in terms of looking after someone whose mother is away. This is, this is looking after children who have problems but who, by and large, do not live in the institution. Although there are very few, I understand, who still live there, who have nowhere else to, to, to, to live. That's it. This is the building that I lived in for most of my stay in the orphanage, from 1944 until 1946. As you can see, there is a, there is a sheep there. In the orphanages, they did have sheeps and pigs and chickens. And they were raised there to-- as an income-producing venture. The sheeps were a real nuisance, because they leave quite a mess. And as the war progressed and we got more and more hungry, this is the field where we would go looking for dandelions and all the-- which we would eat. And the mess that the sheep made didn't make it easy for us to find these dandelions. But nevertheless, we-- when you're hungry and there is a possibility of food, nothing can hold you back. And we regularly-- there was no problem with weeds. We ate them. This is a picture of some of the older children who were in the orphanage who were in my division. I am on the right in the second row, the middle child with the white collar and wearing a coat. This is a picture taken in front of my building with-- the lady wearing the hat and a coat was the woman in charge of our division. She had devoted her entire life to this institution. She was, at this time, a member of the underground. We didn't know it, but we hated her for stealing our food. We thought she took it for herself. Later we learned she took it for the American flyers she rescued and hid until they could be sent back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know her name? Yeah, Gabrielle Tillman.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of myself taken when I was 14 years old. I vividly recall that somebody's father took a picture of, of all the children who happened to be in the room at the time he was visiting with his child. I don't remember who the child was. But I'm wearing an apron. We always wore aprons. We had an apron for-- to wear in the house. And we had an apron-- those who went to school wore, wore a school apron. We only changed clothes once a week, so it was important to be covered to save our clothes for the seven days that we wore them. This is a picture of my mother on the right, Bella Strauss, and myself on the left, walking on a street in Brussels. This actually is a dress that my mother came back with and the shoes that she was wearing when she came back. I guess for a while, those were the only clothes she had. And-- so that-- this was taken in 1945 sometime after she arrived. It's obviously summer since we're not wearing coats. But we're carrying them. Must have been taken on a Sunday, when I was a-- when was allowed to go out with her. This is a picture of my mother, taken probably in early 1946 or late '45. This was a passport picture. She didn't really have a passport. She had a what's called a [FRENCH], allowing somebody to pass a, a, to pass a border. She didn't have any documents to prove anything at this point in her life, and so she could not obtain a legal passport. But she did have enough documentation to allow to come to the United States. And this is one of the pictures which she had already signed, but didn't have to use. She, I guess, had too many. And this was a leftover one. She's looking pretty good. Her dress-- I recall very vividly, my father sent some fabric. I guess he couldn't send dresses. He wasn't sure of her size. And she had this dress made with this fabric that came. And it's basically what she wore from then on. This is a picture of my three daughters, taken about 13 years ago. My youngest daughter, Naomi, is on the left. The middle one is Judy, my oldest daughter, the one who was born in Aberdeen. Naomi was born in Toronto. And on the right is my middle daughter, Debbie, who was born in Washington. We do have an international family, as you can see. This is a picture of two of my daughters with their husbands and children. On the left is Carl, who is Judy's husband. Judy is the girl kneeling with-- wearing the blue blouse. In the back is my son-in-law, Danny Bachmann, and his wife Debbie, my middle daughter. In the middle on the left is Jesse, my grandson, who is now 13 and a half. Next to him is his sister, Melissa, who is now 11. And the little one on that picture is Hannah, who there was probably around two years old. One grandchild is missing. I'm very proud of all of them. This, this is Miriam, Debbie's youngest child, on the left, my middle daughter's youngest child. And on the right is her sister, Hannah, Who there probably around two and five, something like that, two and a half and five years old. I can't wait to see all of them. And they're all coming here for Passover. And we're going to-- we just can't wait. Some, actually, we're seeing next week. This is a birth certificate issued by the Nazis. You can see, on the-- in the left hand corner, the swastika. The top line has my name, Hilde Margret Sara Strauss. I-- Sara was not really my name. It-- I-- all females in Germany were forced to adopt the name Sara as part of their name. And all males had to adopt the name Israel. Every time I see this piece of paper, I still shudder because it brings back this whole horrible experience. This is a, another German birth certificate that I obtained much later on, after the, after the war ended. And you can see that my name, my first name is now Hilde, and my middle name is Margret. And the name Sarah has disappeared. It also shows that I was born in Frankfurt am Main on the 29th of January, 1930, and that my, my family name is Strauss, and that my father is Adolph, and my mother, Bella, and that she was born Lehmann. Don't know why you need all that information on a birth certificate, but-- and it also tells you that my father was a salesman.
